message:
fix(fcp-host): validate JSON-RPC response ids + poison transport on I/O error

Addresses a dispatcher misroute in the `fcp-host` subprocess connector
(bead flywheel_connectors-mostw). Previously `ConnectorProcessRunner::request`
wrote the request envelope and then returned whatever the next line on
stdout parsed to, without looking at the `id` field. If a prior call
timed out, the subprocess could still flush its late reply afterwards;
the next caller would then receive that stale response as if it were
their own, leaking result data across operations and breaking
per-operation audit.

This commit reworks the runner to:

1. Mint a per-request `id` of the form "{epoch}:{seq}" inside
   `ConnectorRpcRequest` and send it as the JSON-RPC id. The runner now
   owns id generation; `SubprocessConnector::rpc` only carries a
   `method` + `params` pair across the channel (new
   `ConnectorRpcRequest { method, params, response_tx }` shape).

2. Verify `response.id` matches the expected id. A match yields the
   response. A mismatch whose epoch-prefix is numerically smaller than
   the current epoch is logged at WARN and silently discarded (it is a
   known-late reply from a previously timed-out request); the read loop
   keeps reading until the correct id arrives or the per-call timeout
   expires. Any other mismatch — including missing id or unparseable
   epoch — is a hard desync and surfaces
   `connector_transport_desynchronized_error`.

3. Track a `poisoned: bool` flag on the runner. Non-timeout I/O errors
   (write failure, read failure that is not TimedOut) flip `poisoned`
   on, and every subsequent `request*` call short-circuits with
   `connector_transport_poisoned_error` ("restart connector before
   issuing another RPC"). This prevents the runner from being reused in
   a half-broken state where framing alignment has been lost.

4. Bump `epoch` whenever a read times out so that any late reply to the
   abandoned request is classified as stale on the next call. Reads run
   inside a `loop { read_json(remaining) ... }` driven by an `Instant`
   budget so that draining one late reply doesn't silently extend the
   call's wallclock deadline beyond `io_timeout`.

Supporting changes:

- `send_json` / `read_json` now take the `io_timeout: Duration`
  per-call instead of reading the module-level `CONNECTOR_RPC_IO_TIMEOUT`
  constant, which lets callers (tests, long-running ops) customize the
  deadline while still using the shared framing and error path.
- New public `request_with_timeout` alongside `request` so existing
  callers keep the default timeout.
- `connector_io_timeout_error` now takes the effective timeout as a
  parameter so error messages reflect the real deadline rather than the
  constant.
- New `ResponseIdDisposition { Matched, StaleEpoch }` enum + private
  `validate_response_id` helper make the id-match logic testable in
  isolation and keep the async loop readable.

The existing RPC queue capacity (`CONNECTOR_RPC_QUEUE_CAPACITY = 64`)
and default I/O timeout (`CONNECTOR_RPC_IO_TIMEOUT = 10s`) are unchanged.
No behavioural change for well-behaved connectors; misbehaving ones now
fail closed instead of leaking replies across boundaries.
